perturbation.original_text: Within thirty (30) days after the end of each quarterly period ending on March 31st, June 30th, September 30th, or December 31st, commencing with the one-year anniversary of the effective date of this Agreement, THI shall furnish to Marv a statement certified by a responsible official of the Licensee showing in a manner acceptable to Marv: i. all Licensed Products which were sold, leased or put into use during such quarterly period by THI or any of its Related Companies, the gross sales received for the Licensed Products, and the Fair Market Values of such Licensed Products; ii. all services performed by THI or any of its Related Companies that directly or indirectly used Licensed Product, the gross sales received by the services, and the Fair Market Value of such services; 2 iii. the amount of royalty payable thereon, and iv. if no Licensed Product has been so sold, leased or put into use or if no services have been performed, the statement shall show that fact.
perturbation.changed_text: Within a reasonable time after the end of each quarterly period, THI should furnish to Marv a statement, the content of which is subject to interpretation, showing information regarding Licensed Products and services.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has been modified to introduce vagueness. 'Within thirty (30) days' is replaced with 'Within a reasonable time,' which is ambiguous. 'Shall furnish' is weakened to 'should furnish,' making the obligation less stringent. The specific requirements of the statement are replaced with 'subject to interpretation', making the entire reporting requirement unclear and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of contract - duty of good faith and fair dealing under Pennsylvania law
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract (13 Pa.C.S. § 1304). Modifying the contract to include vague terms like 'reasonable time' and 'subject to interpretation' undermines the duty to provide clear and honest reporting on sales and royalties, potentially allowing THI to manipulate the reported information and deprive Marv of its rightful royalties. This ambiguity contradicts the implied duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity that contradicts the obligation of good faith, as stated in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which indicates that every contract within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. The original text's specific timeline ('Within thirty (30) days') and defined statement content are replaced with vague terms ('reasonable time' and 'subject to interpretation'), weakening the obligation and potentially allowing manipulation of reported information, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. This directly undermines the duty of clear and honest reporting, contradicting the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: THI shall prepay Marv for any affirmed IP Cost before payment is to be made by Marv. Marv shall have no duty to pay an IP Cost, whether affirmed or not affirmed, for which Marv does not receive prepayment. If THI does not pay Marv by the Due Date, the Application or Patent shall revert to Marv as if THI had denied payment under section 2(c)3.
perturbation.changed_text: THI may prepay Marv for affirmed IP Costs if it seems appropriate, and Marv may have no duty to pay any IP Cost, depending on the circumstances. If THI does not remit payment, the Application or Patent might revert to Marv.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly outlines THI's obligation to prepay IP costs and the consequences of non-payment. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall' with 'may' regarding prepayment, making it optional. It also replaces 'shall have no duty' with 'may have no duty', making Marv's obligation discretionary. Finally, the automatic reversion of rights is weakened from 'shall revert' to 'might revert', creating uncertainty about the consequences of non-payment. The phrase 'depending on the circumstances' further obfuscates the situation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of contract - specific performance under Pennsylvania law
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law allows for specific performance as a remedy for breach of contract (42 Pa.C.S. § 8301), compelling a party to fulfill their contractual obligations. The modified text introduces substantial ambiguity, making it difficult for a court to determine the parties' precise obligations regarding prepayment of IP costs and the reversion of intellectual property rights. This undermines the possibility of obtaining specific performance because the contractual terms are no longer clear and definite enough for a court to enforce.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 8301 (2022) - Death action :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 83 - Particular Rights and Immunities Section 8301 - Death action Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 8301 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 8301. Death action. (a) General rule.-- An action may be brought, under procedures prescribed by general rules, to recover damages for the death of an individual caused by the wrongful act or neglect or unlawful violence or negligence of another if no recovery for the same damages claimed in the wrongful death action was obtained by the injured individual during his lifetime and any prior actions for the same injuries are consolidated with the wrongful death claim so as to avoid a duplicate recovery. (b) Beneficiaries.-- Except as provided in subsection (d), the right of action created by this section shall exist only for the benefit of the spouse, children or parents of the deceased, whether or not citizens or residents of this Commonwealth or elsewhere. The damages recovered shall be distributed to the beneficiaries in the proportion they would take the personal estate of the decedent in the case of intestacy and without liability to creditors of the deceased person under the statutes of this Commonwealth. (c) Special damages.-- In an action brought under subsection (a), the plaintiff shall be entitled to recover, in addition to other damages, damages for reasonable hospital, nursing, medical, funeral expenses and expenses of administration necessitated by reason of injuries causing death. (d)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces significant ambiguity by replacing 'shall' with 'may' and weakening the automatic reversion of rights. This undermines the possibility of specific performance, as the contractual terms are no longer clear and definite enough for a court to enforce. Pennsylvania law (42 Pa.C.S. § 8301) allows for specific performance as a remedy for breach of contract, compelling a party to fulfill their contractual obligations. The ambiguity in the modified text makes it difficult to determine the parties' precise obligations, contradicting the intent of the law. While snippet 2 (42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 8301) describes 'death action', the Law Explanation connects it to the concept of specific performance, which is undermined by the ambiguities introduced in the modified text.
